Umar Mohd Rizal

### Part 1

I first started by building out the circuit in Figure 5.

![](data:image/jpeg;base64,)

![](data:image/png;base64,)

It’s assumed that the power supply will go across the red rail and ground will go across the blue rail. The gate of the MOSFET will be driven by a function generator.

Computation Assignment:

To get a 5V from 3V, you need a duty cycle of 
$$D = 1-(3/5) = 0.4$$
The period $T$ relies on the frequency of switching at 10 kHZ:
$$T=1/f=0.1\text{ ms}$$
The average current through the inductor should be affected by the duty cycle (because power conservation):
$$i_L)_{av}=2.5/(1-0.4)=4.167\text{ mA}$$
The max can then be found by taking the total approximate change in current between switching stages ($\Delta i=VDT/L$), halving it and adding it to the average, given that the changes are approximately linear.
$$i_L)_{max}=i_L)_{av}+VDT/2L=4.167+1.818=5.985\text{ mA}$$
The converse can be found for the minimum.
$$i_L)_{min}=2.349\text{ mA}$$
$$L_{min}=(3)(0.4)(0.1)/8.333=14.4\text{ mH}$$
$$P_{OUT}=VI=12.5\text{ mW}$$
The ripple can be derived via the following mindset (should be the same on or off...)
> $i=C\partial_t V$
> $i(\Delta t)=C\Delta V$
> $\Delta V=i(\Delta t)/C$
> $\Delta V_{ON}=-i_{LOAD}(DT)/C=-I_{LOAD}D/Cf$
$$V_{ripple}=3.03\text{ mV}$$

And allegedly, I'm gonna find the series resistance of the capacitor in lab.
$$\Delta V_{OUT})_{ESR}=(5.985)(R_{ESR})$$
### Part 2

Using the Waveform generator, produce a 0 to 8V square wave signal to drive the gate. You should be able to see Vout, but I don't have a waveform generator here.

Then we're meant to find the actual D, and the gain given the calculated D. This discrepancy leads to the capacitor series resistance.

I will update this with more information.
### Part 3

Then you make the clock circuit using the LM555CN Timer (555 timer). No idea how it works, but the potentiometer lets you choose a duty cycle.
